2606.04103.title: The Differentiable Auditory Loop (DAL): An ML Framework for Hyper-Personalized Hearing Aids
2606.04103.paper_summary: Conventional hearing aids rely on fixed, frequency-dependent amplification and compression to manage reduced sensitivity, which often fails to provide sufficient listening support in complex environments, such as situations with multiple speakers (the ``cocktail party'' problem). To more comprehensively address the underlying encoding dysfunctions of hearing loss, we introduce the Differentiable Auditory Loop (DAL), a new open-source framework for personalized hearing aid design and fitting. Our first implementation of DAL incorporates CARFAC, a differentiable model of human cochlear function, which we ported to JAX, to optimize a deep neural network to match impaired auditory neural activity patterns with a normal-hearing reference. To build a hearing aid with the fine-grained spectro-temporal signal processing required, we adopt SEANet, a waveform-to-waveform fully convolutional UNet generator. We fine-tune the network by comparing the outputs of a CARFAC model fitted to normal hearing with that of a CARFAC model fitted to match each subject's individual hearing impairment. The comparison is done using loss functions derived from the respective CARFAC neural activity pattern (NAP) outputs and stabilized auditory images (SAIs), the latter providing a 2D representation that captures phase-insensitive temporal structure in the auditory nerve output. Through gradient descent, the SEANet model learns to both denoise the input and compensate for the hearing loss modelled by the impaired CARFAC model. Across neural-representation and signal-fidelity metrics, the DAL-optimized SEANet model outperformed the tested master hearing aid (MHA) baselines. The DAL framework provides a practical path toward model-based, machine-learning-driven personalization of hearing aid signal processing. Next steps include hardware deployment to enable real-world clinical testing.
2606.04103.paper_summary_zh: 傳統的助聽器依賴固定的、頻率依賴的增益和壓縮來管理靈敏度降低，但在複雜環境中，如多位講者的情況（即“雞尾酒會”問題），這通常無法提供足夠的聽力支持。為了更全面地解決聽力損失的基本編碼功能障礙，我們引入了可微分聽覺迴路（Differentiable Auditory Loop, DAL），這是一個新的開源框架，用於個性化助聽器的設計和調整。我們的第一個DAL實現包含了CARFAC，這是一個可微分的人類耳蝸功能模型，我們將其移植到JAX，以優化深度神經網絡，使其能夠將受損的聽覺神經活動模式與正常聽力的參考進行匹配。為了構建一個具備細緻頻譜-時間信號處理能力的助聽器，我們採用了SEANet，一種波形到波形的全卷積UNet生成器。我們通過比較適合正常聽力的CARFAC模型的輸出與適合每位受試者個別聽力損失的CARFAC模型的輸出來微調網絡。比較是使用來自各自CARFAC神經活動模式（NAP）輸出和穩定聽覺影像（SAI）的損失函數進行的，後者提供了一種2D表示，捕捉了聽神經輸出中的相位不敏感時間結構。通過梯度下降，SEANet模型學會了去噪輸入並補償由受損的CARFAC模型所模擬的聽力損失。在神經表示和信號保真度指標中，經DAL優化的SEANet模型超越了測試的主助聽器（MHA）基準。DAL框架提供了一條實用的途徑，朝向基於模型的、機器學習驅動的助聽器信號處理個性化。接下來的步驟包括硬體部署，以便進行現實世界的臨床測試。
2606.04103.author: Alejandro Ballesta Rosen et.al.
2606.04103.authors: Alejandro Ballesta Rosen, Jason Mikiel-Hunter, Julian Maclaren, Jack Collins, Richard F. Lyon, Simon Carlile
